planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–12 weeks before fall planting (August–September in zone 9) in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; carnations are outstanding cool-season flowers in zone 9, blooming prolifically from winter through spring before summer heat stops them — treat as a winter annual in zone 9.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–12 weeks before transplanting; plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun. Dianthus caryophyllus (carnation or clove pink) is the florist's carnation — one of the world's most commercially important cut flowers, comprising billions of stems in the global cut flower trade annually alongside roses and chrysanthemums. The classic double-flowered, fringed-petal blooms in red, pink, white, yellow, orange, purple, and bicolor shades have been cultivated for over 2,000 years; the spicy-sweet, clove-like fragrance of old-fashioned varieties is one of the most beloved flower scents. Modern commercial carnations are often scentless but long-lasting; old-fashioned cottage garden varieties retain the characteristic clove fragrance. Carnations are also edible flowers — the petals (with the bitter white base removed) are sweet and mildly spicy, used to flavor wines, liqueurs (Chartreuse), and desserts; they are the traditional garnish for some Spanish and Greek dishes. In zone 9, carnations are outstanding winter-spring bloomers; plant in fall for a February–May flower season. Standard (tall) types need staking; compact border carnations are self-supporting.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cut Flowers
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Annuals
North Carolina State Extension — Carnation Production

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 2–3 inches deep in spring after soil warms to 65°F or above in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; Siam tulip produces stunning upright flower spikes with pink bracts in summer and thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast.

directions: Plant rhizomes 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun after soil warms; rhizomes go dormant in winter. Curcuma alismatifolia (Siam tulip) is a Thai and Southeast Asian tropical ginger producing one of the most beautiful and unusual flower structures in the tropics — an upright, tulip-shaped cluster of colorful, papery bracts (modified leaves) in pink, lavender, white, or bicolor (pink and white) with small, insignificant flowers hidden within, atop a bare stalk 18–24 inches tall. The flowers resemble a stylized tulip from a distance but are actually inflorescences with showy bracts rather than true petals. Plants emerge from dormant rhizomes in late spring, bloom in summer, and go dormant again after fall — a clean growth cycle suited to Gulf Coast gardens. As a cut flower, Siam tulip is extremely long-lasting (2–3 weeks in a vase). It is one of Thailand's most important commercial cut flower crops and is widely grown in tropical floriculture worldwide. In zone 8–11, the rhizomes can be left in the ground year-round; in colder zones, lift and store at 55°F. Excellent in container plantings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Bulbs
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Bulbs

common_name: Elephant Foot Yam (Whitespot Giant Arum, Suran)

planting.note: Plant corm divisions or small offsets in spring after soil warms to 65°F or above in well-drained, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; elephant foot yam produces the second-largest underground corm of any cultivated plant (second only to giant taro) and is an important food crop throughout South and Southeast Asia.

directions: Plant corm sections in well-drained, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade in spring after soil warms; lift corms before first frost in zone 9. Amorphophallus paeoniifolius (elephant foot yam or suran) is a tropical Asian aroid producing a single, enormous, deeply divided, umbrella-like leaf (resembling a tropical fern frond writ large) on a mottled green-and-grey petiole, and a massive underground corm that can reach 20–30 lbs in a single season. The corm is an important starchy food crop throughout South and Southeast Asia — particularly in India and Sri Lanka, where it is cooked as a curry vegetable, fried in oil, and made into a popular Bengal-region dish. The flesh is white, somewhat mucilaginous (like taro), with a mild, slightly bitter flavor that is neutralized by cooking with acidic ingredients like tamarind. Like all amorphophallus species, the occasional flower is an extraordinary, though briefly malodorous structure — a large, purple-maroon spathe and spadix with the characteristic carrion-flower smell that attracts pollinators. The plant is entirely ornamental in terms of foliage, with a single dramatic leaf reaching 4–6 feet across. Must be cooked thoroughly before eating — raw corms contain irritating calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Root Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in moist to moderately dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Turkey tangle fogfruit is a native North American groundcover that spreads rapidly by stolons and is outstanding for wildlife habitat — it is a critical larval host plant for three species of buckeye and cloudywing butterfly caterpillars.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist to dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; fogfruit spreads by stolons and self-seeds to form a dense mat. Phyla nodiflora (turkey tangle fogfruit or frogfruit) is a low-growing, mat-forming native perennial groundcover of the southeastern United States, Gulf Coastal Plain, and tropical Americas — one of the most important native groundcovers for butterfly habitat in the Gulf South. The plant produces trailing, rooting stems and tiny, oval leaves, with small (1/4-inch), pink-to-lilac-white flower clusters on short stalks throughout the warm season. What makes fogfruit ecologically significant is its role as a larval host plant: it is the primary or sole caterpillar host plant for Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia), White Peacock (Anartia jatrophae), and Phaon Crescent (Phyciodes phaon) butterflies in the Gulf South — caterpillars of these abundant butterflies feed almost exclusively on fogfruit leaves. As a no-mow lawn substitute or habitat groundcover, fogfruit is outstanding for hot, sunny, moist-to-dry situations. It is also occasionally used as a lawn grass substitute in India and South Asia (karpooravalli) for its drought tolerance.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Groundcovers

planting.note: Direct sow or plant established divisions in spring in very well-drained, lean, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, coastal soil in full sun; seaside sandmat is native to coastal dunes, salt flats, and beach margins of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic Coast and is one of the most salt, drought, and heat-tolerant native groundcovers for coastal landscapes.

directions: Sow seeds or plant divisions in very well-drained, lean, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; seaside sandmat is low-maintenance in coastal sandy soils. Euphorbia mesembryanthemifolia (seaside sandmat or coastal sandmat, formerly Chamaesyce mesembryanthemifolia) is a low-growing, mat-forming native succulent perennial of sandy Atlantic and Gulf Coast beaches, dunes, and coastal salt flats — one of the most salt, drought, and heat-tolerant native groundcovers for challenging coastal landscapes. The plant produces dense, prostrate mats of small, fleshy, succulent-textured, grey-green leaves on trailing stems with tiny, inconspicuous flowers, forming a spreading carpet just 2–4 inches tall. As a coastal revegetation and native beach stabilization plant, it is useful for controlling dune erosion and establishing native cover in infertile, salty, windy coastal conditions where almost nothing else grows. In native plant landscaping, it can be used as a low-maintenance, salt-tolerant groundcover for beach-front or oceanside properties as an alternative to invasive non-native beach groundcovers. The milky latex sap (as in all Euphorbia) is a mild irritant; CAUTION if skin-sensitive.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Coastal Native Plants
